message.content:
Adversaries may exploit Regsvr32, a command-line utility used to register/unregister OLE controls and DLLs on Windows systems, to execute malicious code while evading detection. This technique leverages the trusted nature of the Regsvr32 binary, which is often signed by Microsoft, to bypass security tools that may not scrutinize its execution due to allowlists or false positives. Regsvr32 can load COM scriptlets from external URLs without modifying the Registry, a method known as "Squiblydoo," used in various campaigns, including those targeting governments.

The impact of this technique includes unauthorized code execution, persistence, and potential data exfiltration. Common adversary tactics involve using Regsvr32 to execute backdoors, download and run payloads, and establish persistence through COM object hijacking. Notable groups such as APT19, APT32, Cobalt Group, and Lazarus Group have employed this method.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the use of Regsvr32, implementing application whitelisting, and employing behavioral analysis to detect unusual Regsvr32 activity. Additionally, network monitoring can help identify suspicious external scriptlet loading activities.
